sections.heading: Project Overview
sections.content:
This project aimed to design, construct and test a direct current (DC) motor capable of lifting a R5 coin to a vertical height of one metre.

The motor converted electrical energy into mechanical energy through the interaction of electromagnetic induction.

A sensitivity analysis was performed by varying the current and the gap size between the magnets to determine their effects on torque and motor speed.

The results showed that the motor operated most effectively at a current of 2.9A and a magnet gap of 33mm.

This project illustrated how electromagnetism can be implemented to create a functional motor, highlighting the trade-offs between current consumption, coil count and motor efficiency. Ultimately, the project successfully met the aim of constructing a motor capable of lifting the R5 coin to a height of one metre.

sections.heading: My Contribution
sections.content: The workload on this project was evenly split between me and my team member. I built the final optimally designed spinning motor piece containing the copper coil. We then worked together adjusting each piece of the motor until it worked as required.

sections.heading: Implementation & Technical Details
sections.content:
This project involved the design, construction, and optimisation of a custom direct current (DC) motor capable of lifting a R5 coin vertically by one metre.

The motor converted electrical energy into mechanical motion through electromagnetic induction, utilising a custom-built rotor, stator, split-ring commutator, permanent magnets, and copper wire coils.

A sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate the effects of current and magnet spacing on motor performance, revealing an optimal operating point of approximately 2.9 A with a 33 mm magnet gap.

Multiple design iterations were tested to improve efficiency, with the final design incorporating additional coils, reduced rotor weight, and improved magnet positioning to maximise torque and reduce required operating voltage.

The project demonstrated practical application of electromagnetic principles, experimental optimisation techniques, CAD-based design iteration, and electromechanical system construction, ultimately producing a functional motor that successfully met the lifting requirements.
